q: Which open standard defines the on-disk format and runtime behavior of container images?
expl: OCI standardizes the image format (image-spec) and runtime behavior (runtime-spec) so any compliant builder produces images any compliant runtime can run. CNI standardizes pod networking plugins (Calico, Cilium, ...). CSI standardizes storage drivers. CRI is the gRPC interface kubelet uses to talk to runtimes like containerd/CRI-O — it does not define the image format itself.

q: Resilience patterns for distributed systems include:
opts: Synchronous retries with no backoff or jitter
Circuit breakers, exponential backoff with jitter, and graceful degradation
Tight coupling to dependencies so failures fail fast and loudly
Global locks on shared state to serialize all requests
expl: Circuit breakers stop hammering a failing dependency, exponential backoff with jitter prevents thundering-herd retries, and graceful degradation keeps core functionality working when non-critical dependencies fail. A causes retry storms that worsen outages. C amplifies blast radius rather than containing it. D introduces a single point of contention and is an availability anti-pattern in distributed systems.

q: Immutable infrastructure means:
opts: Infrastructure is patched in place after deployment
Components are replaced entirely rather than modified — typically via new images
Infrastructure is tied to specific physical hardware and cannot be moved
Configuration drift is corrected by SSHing in and running scripts
expl: Immutable infrastructure means you replace, not modify. Containers make this natural: build a new image, deploy it, kill the old pod. Benefits: reproducibility, easier rollback, no config drift. A and D both describe mutable approaches (in-place patching, manual remediation); C confuses immutability with hardware coupling.

q: The "three pillars" of observability are:
expl: Metrics (numeric time-series), Logs (timestamped events), Traces (request paths through distributed systems). Observability is the goal; these are the signals that enable it.

q: Prometheus is primarily:
opts: A time-series database with a pull-based monitoring and alerting model
expl: Prometheus (CNCF graduated) scrapes HTTP metrics endpoints at intervals, stores them in a TSDB, and queries them with PromQL. Alertmanager handles routing. It is the de facto K8s metrics standard.

q: What is OpenTelemetry?
opts: A vendor-neutral observability framework (APIs, SDKs, collectors) for metrics, logs, and traces
A time-series database and metrics backend that replaces Prometheus
A log aggregation agent that ships container stdout to cloud storage
A distributed tracing UI for visualizing service dependency graphs
expl: OpenTelemetry (CNCF) decouples instrumentation from backends: instrument your app once with OTel APIs/SDKs, then route metrics, logs, and traces through the OTel Collector to backends like Prometheus, Jaeger, Tempo, or commercial vendors. It is not itself a TSDB (B), a log shipper (C), or a tracing UI (D) — it is the standardized telemetry pipeline that feeds those tools.

q: An SLO (Service Level Objective) is:
opts: An internal target for a service level indicator (e.g., 99.9% success over 30 days)
expl: SLI = what you measure. SLO = target for SLIs (internal). SLA = external contract (usually looser than SLO, with penalties). Error budget = allowable unreliability = 100% − SLO.

q: Which CNCF project is for distributed tracing?
expl: Jaeger (CNCF graduated, originally from Uber) visualizes how requests flow across services. OpenTelemetry commonly feeds trace data to Jaeger or to Grafana Tempo.

q: GitOps is best described as:
opts: An operational pattern where Git is the source of truth and an operator continuously reconciles the cluster
A way to store images in Git
expl: Git holds desired state. Argo CD or Flux watches the repo and reconciles cluster state to match. Changes happen via pull request. Benefits: auditability, rollback via Git revert, fully declarative.

q: Argo CD and Flux are examples of:
opts: GitOps continuous delivery tools for Kubernetes
expl: Both are CNCF projects. Argo CD has a rich web UI and app-centric model; Flux is a collection of small controllers composable via the GitOps Toolkit. Either is fine for KCNA.

q: A canary deployment:
opts: Replaces all instances at once in a single cutover
Routes a small share of traffic to the new version, increasing if healthy
Maintains two full environments and switches all traffic at cutover
Replaces pods one at a time without any traffic shaping
expl: Canary = gradual traffic shift with observation. If error rate or latency degrades on the small canary slice, you roll back before most users are affected. A describes a recreate strategy; C describes blue/green (two full environments, atomic switch); D describes a rolling update (instance-by-instance, but every healthy pod gets full traffic share).

q: What is Helm?
opts: A lightweight Kubernetes distribution targeted at edge clusters
A package manager for Kubernetes that uses templated, parameterized charts
A GitOps continuous-delivery operator that reconciles cluster state from a repo
A templating overlay engine for raw YAML that ships built into kubectl
expl: Helm (CNCF graduated) packages Kubernetes manifests into charts with parameterizable values — think apt/brew for Kubernetes. C describes Argo CD or Flux. D describes Kustomize, which uses overlays (not templates) and is invoked via kubectl -k. A is unrelated (e.g., k3s/MicroK8s are distributions, not Helm).

q: What is Tekton?
opts: A service mesh providing mTLS and traffic management between services
A Kubernetes-native CI/CD framework whose pipelines are defined as CRDs and run as pods
A GitOps reconciliation controller that syncs manifests from Git to the cluster
A workflow engine designed primarily for batch and data pipelines
expl: Tekton (CNCF) defines Tasks, Pipelines, and PipelineRuns as Kubernetes CRDs; each step runs in its own pod, making CI/CD a first-class cluster workload. A describes Istio/Linkerd. C describes Argo CD/Flux (GitOps), which is complementary to but distinct from CI. D describes Argo Workflows, which is more general-purpose and commonly used for data/batch jobs rather than build/test/deploy pipelines.

q: Which architectural component ensures that the actual state of cluster resources converges towards the desired state defined in their specifications?
expl: Controllers implement reconciliation loops. They watch the desired state via the API server and interact with resources to make the current state match the desired state (e.g., ReplicaSet controller ensuring the right number of Pods exist).

q: A Pod specification includes two containers: one web server and one log shipper. How do these containers communicate efficiently over the network within the Pod?
opts: Via the node's primary IP address
Via localhost and shared network namespace
Via distinct Pod IP addresses assigned to each container
expl: Containers within the same Pod share the same network namespace, so they can reach each other over localhost on any port — no Service, NetworkPolicy, or extra Pod IP is required. A and B route through cluster networking unnecessarily. D is irrelevant — NetworkPolicies gate traffic, they don't enable it. E is wrong because each Pod gets a single IP shared by all its containers, not one per container.

q: What is the primary role of kubelet on a worker node?
opts: To schedule Pods onto the node
To store the state of Pods running on the node
To ensure containers described in PodSpecs assigned to its node are running
To manage network routing rules for Services
To authenticate API requests originating from the node
expl: The kubelet is the primary node agent. It receives PodSpecs from the API server and interacts with the container runtime (e.g., containerd) to start, stop, and manage the containers defined in those Pods, reporting their status back to the control plane.

q: Which Kubernetes resource provides a mechanism for grouping API objects and providing a scope for names?
expl: Namespaces create logical partitions within a cluster, allowing teams or applications to operate independently. Resource names must be unique within a Namespace, but not necessarily across the entire cluster.

q: When interacting with the Kubernetes API server using kubectl, what is the typical format used for defining resource manifests?
expl: While the API server can accept JSON, YAML is the overwhelmingly conventional and human-readable format used in configuration files (manifests) passed to kubectl for creating or updating resources.

q: What core Kubernetes concept allows Deployments to perform rolling updates with zero downtime?
opts: Incrementally replacing Pods managed by ReplicaSets
expl: A Deployment performs rolling updates by creating a new ReplicaSet and gradually scaling it up while scaling down the old ReplicaSet. Pods are replaced incrementally according to maxSurge and maxUnavailable settings, so a healthy set of replicas keeps serving traffic throughout the rollout, achieving zero downtime.

q: How does Kubernetes typically handle the failure of a container within a Pod?
opts: By deleting the entire Pod immediately
By restarting the failed container based on the Pod's restartPolicy
By scheduling a new Pod on a different node
By marking the Node as unschedulable
By alerting the kube-scheduler to find a replacement container
expl: The kubelet monitors container health. If a container fails, the kubelet restarts it according to the Pod's restartPolicy (Always, OnFailure, Never). The Pod itself continues to exist on the same node.

q: What distinguishes a Kubernetes Secret from a ConfigMap?
opts: Secrets are namespaced, ConfigMaps are not
ConfigMaps store configuration data, Secrets store sensitive data (base64 encoded)
Secrets can only be mounted as environment variables, ConfigMaps as files
ConfigMaps are automatically encrypted at rest, Secrets are not
Secrets are immutable once created, ConfigMaps are mutable
expl: Both are used for configuration, but Secrets are intended for sensitive information (passwords, keys) and are stored base64 encoded (not truly encrypted by default in etcd, though). ConfigMaps are for nonsensitive configuration data. Both are namespaced and can be mounted in multiple ways.

q: Which of the following is NOT a standard component of the Kubernetes control plane?
expl: containerd is a container runtime; it typically runs on worker nodes (managed by the kubelet) to handle container execution, not as part of the central control plane.

q: What is the purpose of labels in Kubernetes?
opts: To provide detailed descriptive information about a resource
To define network access rules between resources
To attach identifying metadata for selection and organization
To specify resource requests and limits for containers
expl: Labels are key/value pairs attached to objects (like Pods, Services) used to organize resources and allow users/controllers to select subsets of objects (e.g., a Service selecting Pods with app=backend).

q: When defining a Pod, what does the spec.containers[].image field specify?
opts: The base operating system for the container
The specific container image (and tag) to run
The command to execute inside the container
The resource limits for the container
The network port the container will listen on
expl: This field tells the container runtime which image (e.g., nginx:1.21 or myrepo/myapp:v2.5) to pull from a registry and use to create the container process.

q: If a Pod needs access to specific hardware features available only on certain nodes, which scheduling mechanism is most suitable?
opts: Taints and Tolerations or Node Affinity
expl: Taints applied to nodes repel Pods that don't Tolerate them. Node Affinity allows Pods to express preferences or requirements for nodes based on node labels (which can represent hardware features, location, etc.). Both help ensure Pods land on appropriate nodes.

q: Which API object represents a single point of access to a set of Pods providing the same functionality, acting as an internal load balancer?
expl: A Service defines a logical set of Pods (usually selected by labels) and provides a stable IP address and DNS name. kube-proxy ensures traffic to the Service IP is load-balanced across the healthy backend Pods.

q: What is the relationship between a Deployment and a ReplicaSet?
opts: A Deployment watches ReplicaSets for scaling signals
A Deployment declaratively manages ReplicaSets to orchestrate Pod updates
ReplicaSets are used only for stateful applications, Deployments for stateless
They are independent controllers managing Pod lifecycles
expl: A Deployment provides declarative updates for Pods and ReplicaSets. It creates and manages ReplicaSets, handling rolling updates or rollbacks by manipulating the desired replica count of underlying ReplicaSets. You typically interact with Deployments, not ReplicaSets directly.

q: The Kubernetes API server performs several actions when receiving a request. Which action verifies if the authenticated user is allowed to perform the requested operation?
expl: After Authentication (who are you?), Authorization (e.g., via RBAC) checks if the identified user has the necessary permissions (Role/ClusterRole bindings) to perform the action (e.g., create, get, delete) on the specific resource (e.g., pods) in the target namespace.

q: What is the primary function of etcd within the Kubernetes architecture?
opts: To run the container runtime interface
To schedule Pods across available nodes
To provide reliable, distributed storage for cluster state and configuration
To manage network policies and ingress rules
To execute health checks against running containers
expl: etcd is the consistent key-value store that acts as the source of truth for the entire cluster. All configuration, state, and metadata are persisted here, ensuring consistency across the control plane.

q: Which field in a Pod's specification is crucial for the kubescheduler to determine if a node has sufficient resources?
expl: The resources.requests field (for CPU and memory) indicates the minimum amount of resources the container needs. The scheduler uses this to filter out nodes that cannot meet these requests.

q: What fundamental concept allows Kubernetes controllers to operate effectively without needing constant instructions?
expl: Controllers continuously watch the desired state (from the API server) and the current state (observed in the cluster), taking actions (via the API server) to make the current state match the desired state. This is the core principle of Kubernetes automation.

q: Which OCI (Open Container Initiative) specification defines how container runtimes should execute containers?
expl: The OCI Runtime Specification outlines how to run a container filesystem bundle. Kubernetes uses the Container Runtime Interface (CRI) as an abstraction layer over runtimes like containerd or CRI-O that implement this standard.

q: You need to provide non-sensitive configuration data (like a URL endpoint or logging level) to a Pod. Which resource is most appropriate?
expl: A ConfigMap stores non-sensitive configuration data as key/value pairs. It can be consumed by Pods as environment variables, command-line arguments, or files mounted into a volume, decoupling configuration from container images. Secrets serve the same role for sensitive data; ConfigMaps are the right choice for plain values like URLs or log levels.

q: What does it mean for the Kubernetes API to be "declarative"?
opts: API calls must be made sequentially
Users specify the desired end state, not the steps to reach it
The API only accepts YAML formatted requests
API responses always include the full resource specification
The API can only create resources, not update or delete them
expl: Users declare the desired state of resources (e.g., "I want 3 replicas of this Pod"). Kubernetes controllers then figure out the sequence of actions needed to achieve and maintain that state. Contrast this with imperative approaches ("run container X", "scale up by 1").

q: Which mechanism prevents the kube-scheduler from placing Pods on a Node that is undergoing maintenance or is otherwise unsuitable?
opts: Taints on the Node and Tolerations on the Pod
expl: A Taint applied to a Node repels Pods. Pods can express Tolerations for specific taints, allowing them to be scheduled onto those nodes. This is commonly used to cordon nodes for maintenance or dedicate nodes for specific workloads.

q: Which component translates a Service's virtual IP address into actual Pod IP addresses on each node?
expl: kube-proxy, running on each node, watches Services and Endpoints/EndpointSlices. It programs network rules (e.g., iptables, IPVS) to intercept traffic destined for a Service's ClusterIP and directs it to one of the healthy backend Pod IPs.

q: A StatefulSet is often preferred over a Deployment for running databases. Why?
opts: StatefulSets provide stable, unique network IDs and persistent storage per Pod
Deployments automatically handle database schema migrations
StatefulSets require less CPU and memory resources
expl: StatefulSets provide guarantees crucial for stateful apps: stable network identifiers (e.g., db-0, db-1), stable persistent storage linked to each identity, and ordered, graceful deployment and scaling.

q: What is the primary goal of the Open Container Initiative (OCI)?
opts: To create a commercial container platform
To define open standards around container formats and runtime
To manage the Kubernetes source code repository
To provide free container image hosting
expl: OCI aims to establish common, open, minimal standards for container image formats and container runtime execution to ensure interoperability and prevent fragmentation in the container ecosystem.

q: Which kubectl command is used to view the logs of a running container within a Pod?
expl: kubectl logs fetches the logs (stdout/stderr) from a specified container within a Pod. The -c flag is needed if the Pod has multiple containers.

q: What is the function of an Admission Controller in the Kubernetes API request lifecycle?
opts: To authenticate the user making the request
To mutate or validate API objects before they are persisted in etcd
To schedule the Pod onto a suitable node
To route network traffic to the correct Pod
expl: Admission Controllers intercept API requests after authentication and authorization but before persistence. They can mutate objects (e.g., inject sidecars, set default values) or validate them (e.g., enforce security policies, check resource quotas).

q: Which resource defines constraints on the total amount of compute resources (CPU, memory) that can be consumed within a specific Namespace?
expl: A ResourceQuota limits the aggregate resource consumption (CPU, memory, storage, object counts) within a Namespace, preventing overuse and ensuring fair sharing among users or teams.

q: Consider a Pod that needs to perform a one-time initialization task before its main application container starts. Which container type is best suited for this?
expl: Init Containers run sequentially before the main application containers in a Pod start. They must complete successfully for the main containers to launch, making them ideal for setup tasks like database schema migration, fetching configuration, or waiting for dependencies.

q: What is the purpose of the metadata.ownerReferences field in a Kubernetes object?
opts: To specify the user who created the object
To link the object to its managing controller (e.g., ReplicaSet to Deployment)
To list labels used for selecting the object
To store annotations related to the object's owner
To define the Namespace the object belongs to
expl: ownerReferences create parent-child relationships between objects. This enables cascading deletion (deleting the owner deletes dependents) and helps controllers identify the objects they manage. For example, a ReplicaSet Pod will have an ownerReference pointing to the ReplicaSet.

q: Which statement best describes the relationship between Kubernetes and Docker (as of 2025)?
opts: Kubernetes requires Docker to be installed on all nodes.
Docker is the only container runtime supported by Kubernetes.
Kubernetes primarily interacts with container runtimes via CRI (e.g., containerd, CRI-O), abstracting Docker.
Docker manages the Kubernetes control plane components.
Kubernetes is a component within the Docker Enterprise platform.
expl: Kubernetes deprecated the direct Docker integration (dockershim). It now interacts with OCI-compliant runtimes like containerd (which Docker itself uses) or CRI-O via the Container Runtime Interface (CRI). While Docker might be installed, K8s doesn't use it directly in the same way.

q: If multiple Pods need to share persistent data, which volume type allows simultaneous mounting by multiple Pods (potentially with read/write access)?
opts: A PersistentVolume with an accessMode like ReadWriteMany (RWX)
expl: PersistentVolumes (PVs) represent external storage. Their accessModes define how they can be mounted. ReadWriteOnce (RWO) allows mounting by a single node, while ReadWriteMany (RWX) allows simultaneous read/write mounting by multiple nodes (requires a capable storage backend like NFS).

q: Which control plane component is primarily responsible for running controllers like the Node Controller, Deployment Controller, and Service Controller?
expl: The kube-controller-manager bundles core Kubernetes controllers into a single binary. It runs controllers that handle node lifecycle, workload management (Deployments, ReplicaSets), service endpoints, namespace creation, etc.

q: What is the purpose of a LimitRange object within a Namespace?
opts: To limit the total resource usage of the entire Namespace
To set default resource requests/limits for containers and validate min/max bounds
To restrict network traffic between Pods in the Namespace
To control which users can create resources in the Namespace
To define storage quotas for PersistentVolumeClaims
expl: A LimitRange operates at the container/Pod level within a Namespace. It can enforce minimum/maximum resource constraints and specify default request/limit values if they aren't explicitly set in the Pod spec. ResourceQuota limits the Namespace total.
